query: efgartigimod efficacy and safety in myasthenia gravis

retrieved_docs.title: Type II Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should be aware of the potential for medications such as fluoroquinolones, macrolides, and neuromuscular blocking agents to exacerbate myasthenia gravis. Pyridostigmine improves neuromuscular transmission and is first-line in patients with mild symptoms of myasthenia gravis. Biologics such as rozanolixizumab, IVIG, glucocorticoids, or plasma exchange are used in patients with refractory symptoms or myasthenic crisis. Thymectomy is beneficial in early-onset or thymoma-associated disease. Fc receptor antagonists, such as efgartigimod, and C5 inhibitors, such as eculizumab, represent novel targeted therapies for severe cases. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis treatment research is a rapidly developing field, with multiple new drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the last couple of years. These novel agents include efgartigimod, rozanolixizumab, ravulizumab, and zilucoplan. Efgartigimod and rozanolixizumab are neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) antagonists that lower pathogenic antibodies by enhancing their degradation. Rozanolixizumab is approved for both AChR and MuSK antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. Ravulizumab and zilucoplan are complement inhibitors that block complement-mediated damage at the NMJ, specifically for AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Thrombocytopenia -- Treatment / Management -- Emerging Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Efgartigimod and rozanolixizumab are compounds undergoing testing for use in myasthenia gravis and ITP. These agents are antibody fragments targeting the neonatal Fc-receptor (FcRn), thus inhibiting IgG recycling. Efgartigimod and rozanolixizumab decrease IgG’s half-life, reducing it to normal or subpathogenic levels. Investigators have found response rates of 38.5% with efgartigimod and more than 50% with rozanolixizumab. [40] [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressive Treatment: These are indicated in patients who remain symptomatic even after pyridostigmine treatment. Glucocorticoids (prednisone, prednisolone, and methylprednisolone) and azathioprine are the first-line immunosuppressive agents used in the treatment of MG. Second-line agents include cyclosporine, methotrexate, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, and tacrolimus. These are used when the patient is unresponsive to treatment, has any contraindication to treatment, or intolerability to the use of first-line agents. Recently, various monoclonal antibodies, including rituximab and eculizumab, have been used to treat drug-resistant MG, but data from clinical trials about their efficacy is yet to be documented. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Rituximab -- Indications -- Myasthenia gravis
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA has recently approved 2 biosimilars of rituximab for adult patients. Biosimilars are drugs that have no clinically significant difference from the reference drug. These have a similar structure, safety profile, and function. Developing biosimilars aims to make medicines more accessible by lowering the cost. [2] Rituximab-abbs is approved for use in NHL alone or as part of a combination chemotherapy regimen and CLL in combination with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide. Rituximab-pvvr has approval for use in non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), and microscopic polyangiitis (MPA).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Another treatment approach is to use immunosuppressive therapies, including corticosteroids like prednisone, as a first-line treatment to reduce autoantibody production. Other options are steroid-sparing agents, such as azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil, and the monoclonal antibodies rituximab and eculizumab. Rituximab targets B cells to reduce the production of pathogenic autoantibodies and is particularly effective in MuSK antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. Eculizumab is a complement inhibitor and is used in AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis with refractory symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Efgartigimod and rozanolixizumab are compounds undergoing testing for use in myasthenia gravis as well as in ITP. [5] [19] They are antibody fragments that target the neonatal Fc-receptor (FcRn), thereby inhibiting IgG recycling. They decrease the half-life of IgG, reducing it to normal or subpathogenic levels. Investigators have found response rates of 38% with efgartigimod and 50% with Rozanolixizumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Rituximab -- Indications -- Myasthenia gravis
retrieved_docs.content: In combination with hyaluronidase (rituximab-hyaluronidase human), rituximab was approved by the FDA in 2017. This formulation is available for use in the subcutaneous form, giving the advantage of a faster administration over 5 to 7 minutes instead of prolonged infusion times with IV formulations. This combination is approved for use in chronic lymphocytic leukemia, follicular lymphoma, and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. One limitation of use is that it can only be given after receiving a full dose of a rituximab product by IV infusion. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients diagnosed with LEMS often experience a diminished quality of life due to weakness, autonomic involvement, and potential treatment-related adverse effects. However, LEMS typically responds positively to symptomatic and immunosuppressive therapies. Upon diagnosis, approximately 60% of patients maintain their independence in daily activities, and this proportion increases to 85% within a year of treatment. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In cases where patients continue to have an enduring weakness that does not respond to symptomatic treatments, it becomes necessary to consider immunomodulating or immunosuppressive therapies, some of which are mentioned below.

retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG): IVIG is the primary treatment option for refractory cases. Despite its uncertain mechanism of action, it is believed to function by neutralizing autoantibodies and regulating autoreactive B cells. The usual regimen involves administering 2 g/kg over 2 to 5 days. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Myopathies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Rituximab, anti-CD 20 monoclonal antibodies, is effective in treating myositis and myositis-related interstitial lung disease. Rituximab is preferred over cyclophosphamide by many clinicians due to its better tolerance and adverse effect profile. Pharmacists review medications for appropriateness, dosage, and interactions. They also provide patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The primary care doctor should educate the patient about the precipitating events that could trigger an attack, the treatment modalities available, and the long term complications. The patient should be allowed to choose among the available treatment strategies. The long term treatment of myasthenia gravis requires that the patient be advised about the importance of medication compliance. The family members may be included in the care of the patient once consent is obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and flutikason are both classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroid class, indicating that they belong to the high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid medications. As potent group III corticosteroids, they share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are typically employed in clinical settings where a high‑potency topical steroid is required. Their shared classification guides clinicians in dosing, safety monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making for conditions that benefit from potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1730
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide, flutikason, fluocinolonacetonid, and fluocinonid are all classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroid category. As high‑potency topical corticosteroids, these agents share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly used to treat dermatologic conditions that require potent local therapy. Recognizing that these four drugs belong to the same potency group assists clinicians in selecting appropriate dosing regimens, anticipating similar efficacy, and monitoring for comparable safety concerns such as skin atrophy with prolonged use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: Cykl​ofosfamid, an alkylating chemotherapeutic and immunosuppressive agent, may be used to treat both multiple sclerosis and multiple myeloma, illustrating its therapeutic relevance across a demyelinating autoimmune disorder and a hematologic malignancy. Its capacity to modulate immune activity and inhibit proliferating cells underlies its application in these distinct diseases, emphasizing the importance of versatile treatment options in personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as “triamcinolon,” is classified within the group of moderately potent corticosteroids that are used in various combination formulations (“Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”). As a member of this corticosteroid class, triamcinolone possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that make it a therapeutic option for managing multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By recognizing triamcinolone’s place among moderately potent corticosteroids, clinicians can better understand its mechanism of action, appropriate dosing, and potential side‑effect profile when it is employed to reduce inflammation and disease activity in patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids classified as potent (group III) comprise the agents amcinonide, flutikason, budesonide, fluocinolonacetonide and fluocinonide, each of which is a member of this high‑potency corticosteroid group. These drugs share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly employed in dermatologic, respiratory and other inflammatory conditions where a potent corticosteroid effect is required. Their classification together reflects similar pharmacologic strength, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for patients needing potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Fluokortolon and desoximetason are both classified as potent corticosteroids and are included in the category “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations.” As members of this group, they share the pharmacologic characteristics of strong glucocorticoid agents, providing powerful anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and often being incorporated into various combination drug formulations. Recognizing their membership in this potent corticosteroid class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens and anticipating the potency‑related safety considerations associated with high‑strength glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1604

This grouping reflects their similar pharmacologic strength and informs clinicians about their comparable therapeutic potency, typical indications, and safety considerations when selecting a potent corticosteroid for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and fluocinonid are classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroids, a high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid agents. This classification indicates that both amcinonide and fluocinonid share the pharmacologic characteristics of potent group III corticosteroids, including strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that are commonly utilized in dermatologic therapy for inflammatory skin conditions. Recognizing amcinonide and fluocinonid as members of this potent corticosteroid group helps clinicians select appropriate potency levels, balance therapeutic efficacy with safety, and guide appropriate short‑term use to limit systemic exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1204
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa, fenoprofen, butenafine, and tolnaftate are all contraindicated in patients who have hypersensitivity, meaning that each of these agents should be avoided in individuals with an allergic predisposition to prevent adverse immune reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1748
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressants represent a broad pharmacologic class that includes a variety of agents used to attenuate immune system activity. Members of this class comprise inhibitory interleukin, mycophenolate, mykofenolát mofetil, daklizumab, everolimus, gliotoxin, hydrochloride of mykofenolát mofetilu, koformycin, kyselina mykofenolová, muromonab‑CD3, and takrolimus. Each of these compounds functions to suppress immune responses and is employed in clinical settings such as organ transplantation, treatment of autoimmune disorders, and management of inflammatory conditions, highlighting the diverse therapeutic options available within the immunosuppressant category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids represent a class of corticosteroid medications that includes agents such as prednison and paramethason. Both prednison and paramethason are synthetic glucocorticoid drugs administered systemically to exert potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By belonging to the systemic glucocorticoid category, these agents share common mechanisms of action, pharmacologic properties, and therapeutic indications, making them useful in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Understanding that prednison and paramethason are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate class‑related side effects, and provide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑Aminopyridine is a pharmacologic agent that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By blocking voltage‑gated potassium channels, 4‑aminopyridine can improve nerve conduction and enhance motor function in patients with multiple sclerosis, thereby serving as a therapeutic option to alleviate some of the disease’s neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1703
retrieved_docs.content: Rimexolone, meprednisone, and paramethason are all members of the systemic glucocorticoid class. As systemic glucocorticoids, these agents share the pharmacologic properties typical of glucocorticoid steroids, including potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that are utilized in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Their classification as systemic glucocorticoids indicates that they are administered in a manner that affects the entire body rather than being limited to local or topical sites. Understanding that rimexolone, meprednisone, and paramethason belong to this drug class helps clinicians anticipate their therapeutic actions, potential side‑effect profiles, and appropriate clinical uses.
